title_of_original: SA.20350 – (C 17/2008) (ex NN 1/2006) implemented by the Czech Republic for several regional bus services operators in the Ústí Region
case_description: [1] On the 15th of February 2020, the authorities of the Castilla-La Mancha Region in Spain faced an unexpected disruption in regional bus services [2] after a major operator, Guagua Express S.A., announced its withdrawal from several public service obligation contracts, [3] effective as of the 31st of March, nine months earlier than planned. [4] The region had already held a tender in December 2019 for bus services starting in 2021. [5] After the winning operator signaled that it would not be able to begin operations earlier than scheduled, [6] the government had to find an interim service provider to cover the period until the beginning of 2021. [7] To maintain basic public transport connectivity, the regional government entered into temporary contracts with alternative providers to take over the affected lines. [8] These temporary contracts were awarded without launching a new tender. [9] Instead, the region turned to the second-best bidders from the original procurement process, where possible. [10] The sole award criterion in the procedure had been price per kilometre, [11] and the procedure was open, transparent, and non-discriminatory. [12] In the nine transport zones where such backup bidders existed, [13] contracts were signed for an interim period of nine months, covering the period from 1 April 2020 to 31 December 2020. [14] The operators agreed to provide services under the same terms as those they had offered in the original procurement round, [15] in particular scheduled and reliable transport on specific regional routes. [16] The contracts provided for compensation to cover the estimated loss incurred by each operator in discharging their public service obligations. [17] This loss was defined as the difference between the forecasted operating costs and the expected revenue from passengers, plus a reasonable profit. [18] Profit was limited by regulation to no more than 4 percent of the net operating costs. [19] The regional government required each operator to submit an objective estimate of its loss in advance, based on standard cost categories and local benchmarks. [20] For example, Express Sanchéz Norte S.L. was contracted to operate 620,000 kilometres at an average cost of EUR 2.00 per kilometre, with expected fare revenues of EUR 1.05 per kilometre. [21] The provable loss was therefore EUR 0.95 per kilometre, totaling EUR 589,000. [22] Another provider, Servicios Interurbanos Rapidos S.A., received compensation of EUR 570,000 for operating 600,000 kilometres under similar terms. [23] These figures were supported by internal cost estimates and validated by regional transport auditors. [24] At the end of the contract period, the actual compensation paid to each operator was compared to the forecasted figures. [25] The contracts stipulated that only the lesser of the estimated or actual loss would be reimbursed. [26] Operators were also required to provide evidence of actual costs and revenues. [27] If the real losses exceeded the original estimate, no additional compensation would be paid. [28] However, if unexpected external cost increases occurred, such as a sharp rise in diesel prices due to tax changes, [29] operators could claim a narrowly defined category of unforeseeable costs, subject to approval by the regional finance department. [30] The interim service contracts were financed entirely from the Castilla-La Mancha regional budget, and operators retained all passenger revenue.
gold_parsing: compensation_for_obligation('public service contract', 'Express Sanchéz Norte', 'public transport'). %[16];[21]
compensation_for_obligation('public service contract', 'Servicios Interurbanos Rapidos', 'public transport'). %[16];[22]
adopted_or_administered_by('public service contract', 'government castilla la mancha'). %[7]
public_intra_state_authority('government castilla la mancha', 'castilla la mancha'). %[-]
